critic_raw:
This RNN vs. logistic regression 30-day readmission experiment has several fundamental methodological flaws.

1. RECALL AT 80% PRECISION AS A PRIMARY METRIC IS THRESHOLD-SELECTED ON THE VALIDATION SET, CREATING A CIRCULAR DEPENDENCY. The validation set is used to select model hyperparameters and to determine the decision threshold at which predicted positive cases achieve 80% precision. This means the primary metric is defined by a threshold chosen to satisfy a precision constraint on the validation set. When this threshold is applied to the test set, the test-set precision will not necessarily be 80% -- it will be whatever precision the model achieves at the validation-optimized threshold. Reporting 'recall at 80% precision' on the test set is therefore potentially misleading: the model may achieve the stated recall at a lower or higher precision on the test set.

2. BIDIRECTIONAL LSTM ON HISTORICAL VISIT SEQUENCES USES BACKWARD PASS OVER FUTURE VISITS. The design uses a bidirectional LSTM on sequences of up to 50 prior visits within 12 months preceding the index visit. If 'bidirectional' refers to the standard Bi-LSTM formulation where the backward pass processes the sequence from the last visit to the first, this is architecturally sound -- all 50 visits are in the past relative to the index visit. However, if the sequence is constructed by including the index visit itself as the final element and the backward pass allows the model to condition on the index-visit representation when processing prior visits, this creates a causal issue. The design does not clarify whether the index visit is part of the sequence or separate; this architectural detail determines whether the design is sound.

3. NESTED 5-FOLD CROSS-VALIDATION WITHIN TRAINING SET FOR LOGISTIC REGRESSION VERSUS EARLY STOPPING ON VALIDATION SET FOR LSTM IS ASYMMETRIC TUNING. The logistic regression C parameter is tuned via 5-fold cross-validation within the training set. The LSTM early stopping epoch and decision threshold are tuned on the validation set. These are different tuning paradigms with different data usage patterns. Nested CV within the training set for logistic regression uses all training data for hyperparameter estimation, while the LSTM uses a held-out validation set. The logistic regression effectively has more data for hyperparameter estimation but at the cost of computing cross-validation -- these are not equivalent in the information they use or the variance in the resulting hyperparameter estimate.

4. 200,000 VISITS FROM A SINGLE HEALTHCARE SYSTEM OVER 36 MONTHS MAY CONTAIN REPEATED PATIENTS ACROSS SPLITS. With 200,000 visits over 36 months, a patient who has multiple admissions within the dataset could have their first admission in the training set and a subsequent admission in the test set. The design assigns patients to splits by admission urgency and Charlson Comorbidity Index tertiles within temporal windows -- but this stratification does not prevent the same patient from appearing in multiple splits at different admission dates. If a high-utilization patient (many admissions) appears in both training and test, the model has implicitly seen that patient during training, creating patient-level leakage that is not addressed.

5. FEATURE SET DIFFERENCE BETWEEN LSTM AND LOGISTIC REGRESSION CONFLATES ARCHITECTURE WITH INFORMATION ACCESS. The LSTM receives sequences of up to 50 visits with per-visit primary diagnosis (100 ICD codes), admission type, length of stay, procedure counts, and time-since-prior-visit. The logistic regression receives aggregated counts: number of hospitalizations, number of chronic diagnoses, binary flags for specific diseases, and length of stay. The LSTM has access to the full diagnostic sequence while logistic regression has only aggregated counts. Any observed performance difference conflates the value of sequential modeling with the value of having access to individual visit-level diagnoses vs. aggregated counts. Without a logistic regression on the same full feature set, or an LSTM trained on aggregated features only, the source of any performance gap cannot be isolated.
all_issues_raised: Recall at 80% precision metric is defined by validation-set threshold that will not achieve 80% precision on the test set
Bidirectional LSTM architectural detail regarding index visit inclusion is ambiguous and may create causal contamination
Nested CV for logistic regression vs. validation-set early stopping for LSTM are asymmetric tuning paradigms
High-utilization patients may appear in both training and test splits across their multiple admissions, creating patient-level leakage
Feature set difference between LSTM and logistic regression conflates architecture benefit with information richness
Recall at 80% precision requires threshold portability from validation to test set; test set precision may not be 80% at the selected threshold
Logistic regression uses nested CV for tuning while RNN uses validation-set early stopping; asymmetric tuning strategies confound the comparison
Bidirectional LSTM uses future visits in backward pass; temporal leakage within patient sequences during training
Left-padding with zeros creates unintended positional feature encoding visit history length; spurious proxy for patient chronicity
Small Bi-LSTM may be capacity-insufficient for 130,000-record training set; comparison tests capacity mismatch not sequential modeling advantage
Bidirectional LSTM incorporates future visit information in its backward pass, constituting architectural look-ahead leakage
Recall at 80% precision comparison is between independently-derived operating points for each model, not a fair same-threshold sensitivity comparison
Nested 5-fold CV for logistic regression vs. validation-set early stopping for LSTM creates asymmetric data-efficiency in hyperparameter selection
Left-padding with zeros makes hidden states identical for all patients below 5 visits, erasing individual differences that masking would preserve
12-month lookback window for patients near the temporal split boundary may include observations from across the training/validation boundary
